---
title: "Limitations and Future Work"
---


::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

This is the section a careful reader will read first. The system has a
real, diagnosed retrieval-stage ceiling that caps the headline at
roughly 60%, against a much higher ceiling on questions where
retrieval succeeds. It is not built for cross-document synthesis — the
strongest cell scores 0.233 on that subset. The evaluation uses
structural proxies for citation correctness, not semantic
correctness; "perfect citations" can coexist with prose that
misrepresents what the cited chunks say. The benchmark is small
(70 questions) and hand-crafted, which gives it diagnostic power
but limits its statistical authority. Two claims this project once
held have been retracted on the record. The v2 work-stream
prioritises the retrieval ceiling, since closing it would lift every
other metric.
:::

---

## Limitations of the System {#sec-limits-system}

The v1 system has three structural limits that constrain what it
can be relied on for in production-style use.

### 1. The retrieval ceiling at ~25% miss rate

11 of 44 answerable questions in the N=70 benchmark (25.0%) fail
at the retrieval stage across all four cells of the sweep. Section
5 traced the root cause to query/chunk language asymmetry on
single-vector CLS-pooled dense embeddings. The miss rate is **not
a model-quality issue**: no swap of language model, no prompt
tightening, no temperature setting can help with a question whose
gold chunks are never in the top-k.

The miss rate is also higher than the N=40 sweep suggested.
N=40 showed 3/26 = 11.5%; N=70 showed 11/44 = 25.0%. The
additional questions at N=70 included more cross-document and
more chunk-targeted single-document questions, which surface the
asymmetry more aggressively. The 25% figure is the more honest
number to plan v2 work against.

The operational implication: **headline citation_recall numbers
should be read alongside the `excluding_retrieval_misses` subset
number**, which is the system's actual answer-stage ceiling.
For the production-default cell that is 0.798 — substantially
higher than the 0.598 headline. A reader citing only the
all_answerable number understates the answer-stage quality; a
reader citing only the `excluding_retrieval_misses` number
overstates the end-to-end system. Both numbers belong on the
table.

### 2. Cross-document synthesis is the system's clearest weakness

The cross_document subset (n=10) scores below 0.25 mean
citation_recall in every cell of the sweep. The production-default
ceiling is 0.233 — roughly one gold chunk in four. Even the
strongest cell on this subset is not at a level any operator
should rely on without verification.

`docs/governance.md` commits to this explicitly: cross-document
synthesis is *not* a supported primary use case at v1. An
underwriter using the system to answer "how do Munich Re and Swiss
Re differ on thermal coal exclusions?" should treat the result as
a starting point for manual cross-reading, not as the answer.

Two compounding factors explain the weakness:

- **Retrieval pressure.** Five of the 11 retrieval misses are
  cross-document questions. The multi-issuer retrieval problem is
  upstream of the synthesis problem; even a model with strong
  cross-document reasoning cannot synthesise from chunks it never
  saw.
- **Synthesis difficulty.** Even on the cross-document questions
  where retrieval works, the models score below their within-
  document level. Cross-document synthesis requires the model to
  recognise that two different chunks discuss the same underlying
  topic from different organisational perspectives, which is a
  qualitatively harder reasoning task than within-document
  citation.

### 3. Latency is a constant ~22 seconds on the production default

The production-default Gemma 4 31B IT cell takes a mean of 22.9
seconds per answerable question. This is acceptable for an
underwriting-research workflow where the alternative is a human
spending several minutes finding the same paragraph. It is not
acceptable for an interactive workflow.

A deployment with stricter latency requirements can override the
default to Qwen 3.6 35B-A3B (6.7× faster) via the
`UNDERWRITING_COPILOT_MODEL` environment variable. The cost of the
override is the non-zero hallucination floor (7 on the N=70
sweep, vs 0 on Gemma) and the ~5pp citation recall drop. The
trade-off is documented; the system does not pick a side.

---

## Limitations of the Evaluation {#sec-limits-eval}

The eval has four limits a reviewer should hold in mind when
reading the headline numbers.

### Citation correctness is structural, not semantic

The scorer measures whether the model cited the chunks the
benchmark author designated as gold. It does **not** measure
whether the model's natural-language prose accurately reflects
what those chunks say. The two failure modes the structural
scorer cannot distinguish:

- **A perfectly-cited but semantically wrong answer.** The model
  cites the right chunk but paraphrases its content incorrectly.
  The structural scorer records this as a citation hit; a
  semantic-correctness scorer would record it as a miss.
- **A semantically correct but mis-attributed answer.** The model
  produces correct prose but cites a different chunk than the
  gold set (perhaps a chunk that also supports the claim). The
  structural scorer records this as a citation miss; the answer
  is in fact correct.

LLM-as-judge semantic correctness (Q10.3 in
`docs/open_questions.md`) is the obvious extension. It is deferred
to v2 for two reasons: (a) it would add LLM-call cost to every
eval cell, materially slowing iteration; (b) the judge model is
itself an uncalibrated component that introduces its own error
distribution. Until that lands, the headline citation recall
should be read as a **structural proxy** for semantic correctness,
not as semantic correctness itself.

### Single-seed run; no variance estimate

Each cell of the sweep is a single run. The temperature-0 setting
and pinned RNG seeds make the cell results bit-reproducible *for
the same software state*, but the eval does not measure
run-to-run variance from a re-seeded sweep, nor variance across
plausible benchmark perturbations.

The qualitative findings (the retraction, the within-document
weakening, the Q12 diagnostic) are robust to any plausible
single-seed variance — they all turn on differences of 5–30
percentage points, not on the second decimal place. But **precise
digits past the first decimal should be read with that caveat**.
A reader who hopes to claim Qwen v2's 0.545 is exactly 0.053 below
Gemma v2's 0.598 is reading the noise. The honest claim is "in
the 5pp range, consistent across subsets."

Multi-seed variance is recorded as future work; it is roughly half
a day of eval rerunning plus aggregation code.

### Hand-crafted benchmark is small and biased toward known shapes

The 70-question benchmark was hand-crafted by reading the corpus
chunks and authoring questions whose answers were known. This
gives the benchmark three properties:

- **Authentic question shape.** Questions resemble what an
  underwriter would plausibly ask.
- **Verifiable gold.** Every gold chunk reference was validated
  against the indexed corpus before the sweep ran.
- **Author bias toward known failure modes.** The benchmark
  exercises the failure modes the author *anticipated*. Failure
  modes the author did not anticipate are not tested.

The structural partitions help — single-chunk vs multi-chunk,
within-document vs cross-document, three refusal categories —
but they cannot guarantee coverage of failure modes that did not
occur to the author. A larger, multi-author benchmark would
broaden this. It is recorded as a v2 work item.

### Two-model survey, not a generalisation

The 2×2 sweep tested two specific candidates because they were the
ones that ran cleanly on the oMLX stack at a usable size during
the five-day window. The Day-2 attempt at Gemma 4 12B was blocked
at the `mlx_vlm` layer (Q9, closed-deferred). Other interesting
models — Llama 3 70B variants, smaller Gemma sizes, the Qwen
reasoning variants — were not in the survey.

The headline claim "Gemma 4 31B IT is the production default" is
therefore a within-this-survey claim, not a generalisation that
Gemma is uniformly the best model family for citation-discipline
RAG. A different operator on different hardware should expect to
re-run the D014 sweep against their candidate models, not adopt
the v1 result wholesale.

---

## Limitations of the Corpus {#sec-limits-corpus}

The indexed v1 corpus has four properties that constrain what the
eval results generalise to.

- **Six documents, four issuers.** The corpus is *deep* on each
  document but *shallow* on issuer diversity. PRA is over-
  represented (three documents) relative to EIOPA (one); peer
  reinsurer breadth is limited to two (Munich Re, Swiss Re). A
  corpus that included Hannover Re, Lloyd's, SCOR, or Berkshire
  Hathaway Re would broaden the cross-document subset's coverage
  and likely change the cross_document headline number.
- **All English.** No multilingual testing. BGE-M3 is trained to
  be multilingual; the system's behaviour on French, German, or
  Japanese regulatory text is unobserved.
- **No internal documents indexed.** The three synthetic Sycamore
  Reinsurance documents are drafted but not in the BM25 vocabulary
  or the Qdrant collection. Indexing them is a half-day v2 task
  and would extend the system to a fifth issuer plus internal-
  category documents. The v1 numbers should not be read as if
  the synthetic documents are part of the test surface.
- **Public-only.** A real reinsurer deployment would index
  documents that are not in the public domain. The eval numbers
  are therefore a *floor* for in-scope public-document performance,
  not a prediction of performance on a richer, partly-internal
  corpus.

---

## Two Claims Retracted on the Record {#sec-limits-retractions}

Section 5 narrated the experimental arc that produced each
retraction; this section names both retractions in the limitations
context, so a reader doing a limitations audit can find them
without going back to Section 5.

### Retraction 1 — The Day-2 family-axis citation discipline claim

> *Original claim (Day 2, N=3):* Qwen 3.6 35B-A3B has weaker
> rigid-format citation discipline than Gemma 4 31B IT, in a way
> that looks like a model-family property rather than a prompt-fit
> artefact.
>
> *Status:* **Retracted.** The N=40 D014 sweep showed Qwen v2
> closed the gap to Gemma to 3.2 percentage points on full-sample
> citation_recall, well within the 10pp pre-stated threshold. The
> N=70 extension reaffirmed the retraction at 5.3pp residual gap.
> Most of the Day-2 gap was prompt-fit, not model-family.
>
> *Residual finding:* a small absolute model behaviour difference
> remains on the hallucination axis (Qwen v2: 7; Gemma v2: 0).
> This is real and qualitatively meaningful, but it is not the
> load-bearing finding the Day-2 claim was.

### Retraction 2 — The N=40 within-document parity claim

> *Original claim (Day 3, N=40):* On within-document workloads
> with retrieval working, Gemma 4 31B IT and Qwen 3.6 35B-A3B
> produce equivalent citation recall (both 0.929 on the n=21
> within_document_retrievable subset).
>
> *Status:* **Weakened, not retracted.** The N=70 sweep shows
> Gemma 0.889 vs Qwen 0.833 on the corresponding n=27 subset — a
> 5.6pp gap, consistent with parallel gaps on other within-
> document subsets (single_chunk_retrievable 5.9pp; multi_chunk
> 4.1pp). The qualitative point that v2 closes most of the gap
> survives. The quantitative point that the gap closes to zero
> does not.

Both retractions are recorded in `docs/journal.md` with timestamp
and commit hash. The journal entries are append-only; the
original claims are not edited, the retractions are appended.
This is the project's primary mechanism for accountability under
honest engineering discipline.

---

## Future Work {#sec-limits-future}

The v2 work-stream is organised by which limitation it addresses,
in rough priority order.

### Priority 1 — Close the retrieval ceiling (Q13)

The 25% retrieval miss rate is the single highest-leverage v2
target. Closing it would lift every downstream metric. Q12's
diagnostic identified three remediation paths, recorded as Q13:

- **LLM query rewriting / HyDE.** Pre-retrieval pass that rewrites
  or expands the user query. Targets the diagnosed root cause
  (query/chunk language asymmetry) directly. Estimated effort:
  1–2 hours plus added per-query LLM latency. Probably the right
  *first* v2 experiment because it is cheap and targets the
  diagnosis.
- **Cross-encoder reranker.** Fetch a wider candidate set (say 50)
  then re-rank the top-50 with a cross-encoder model. Targets
  rank-position errors more broadly. Estimated effort: 3–4 hours
  plus reranking latency.
- **BGE-M3 multi-vector channel (Q7 revisit).** Move from
  single-vector MLX to FlagEmbedding for full multi-functionality
  including ColBERT-style late interaction. Most architecturally
  disruptive of the three (breaks D009 MLX-everywhere). Estimated
  effort: 4–8 hours.

The N=70 retrieval-miss list (Section 6, `q001` `q004` `q013`
`q042` `q044` `q046` `q047` `q051` `q053` `q055` `q056`) is the
test set for v2 work. The order of magnitude that matters is "how
many of these eleven the remediation surfaces in top-5", not "how
much the headline number moves," because the same eleven questions
drag the headline.

### Priority 2 — LLM-as-judge semantic correctness (Q10.3)

The structural citation scorer is a proxy for semantic correctness.
A judge LLM that reads the model's prose plus the cited chunks and
scores whether the prose is supported by those chunks would let
the eval distinguish the two failure modes structural scoring
collapses (cite-right-prose-wrong; cite-wrong-prose-right). This
is the single largest extension to the eval's diagnostic power.

The cost is non-trivial: every eval cell adds a judge LLM call;
the judge itself is an uncalibrated component. The right
sequencing is probably to run it on the existing canonical N=70
runs *first*, see how much it shifts the headline numbers, and
then decide whether to integrate it into the standard sweep.

### Priority 3 — Multi-seed variance estimates

Each cell of the current sweep is a single run. Running the sweep
three times under three different RNG seeds (or three randomised
permutations of question order) and reporting cell-level standard
deviations would let the report make calibrated precision claims
("0.598 ± 0.012") rather than the implicit point estimates the
current report uses. Half a day of eval rerunning plus aggregation
code.

### Priority 4 — Index the synthetic Sycamore documents

The three drafted-but-not-indexed Sycamore Reinsurance documents
(`docs/dataset.qmd`, Section 3) are a half-day v2 task. The catch
is that indexing them requires a benchmark refresh: questions
written against the original 4-issuer corpus may now have
candidates in the synthetic documents, changing gold-chunk
correctness. The v1 carve-out is honest accounting; v2 closes it.

### Priority 5 — Corpus and benchmark expansion

A larger corpus (10–15 documents from 6–8 issuers, plus
internal-style documents) and a larger, multi-author benchmark
(say 150 questions) would broaden the cross-document subset and
expose failure modes the v1 author did not anticipate. This is
not a single deliverable; it is a sustained effort and would
plausibly run alongside Priority 1 work for the first several
weeks of any v2 phase.

### Priority 6 — Operational hardening (security and concurrency)

For a production deployment: real authentication on the oMLX
endpoint, multi-user session isolation in the Streamlit UI,
incremental index updates instead of one-shot rebuilds, and an
audit log of every query and its returned `AnswerResult`.
`docs/security.md` scopes this as the v2 security work-stream; it
is operationally important but not a research question.

### Lower priority — GEPA/DSPy systematic prompt optimisation (Q10)

After D014's hand-designed v2 prompt closed most of the family-
axis gap, the case for layering DSPy's GEPA optimiser on top
weakened to "exploratory, not load-bearing." Q10 remains open as
a possible v2 work-stream but no longer carries the load it would
have if the hand-designed v2 prompt had failed.

---

## What Would Change Our Mind {#sec-limits-change}

A handful of specific findings, if they appeared in v2 work, would
change the headline interpretation in this report.

- **A retrieval remediation that lifts `excluding_retrieval_misses`
  above 0.85.** The current ceiling is 0.798. If a HyDE pass or
  cross-encoder reranker pushes this above 0.85 *and* the
  cross_document subset moves above 0.40, the system enters the
  range where end-to-end deployment for narrow research workflows
  starts to look plausible. Currently it does not.

- **LLM-as-judge semantic scoring that shows a Gemma-vs-Qwen
  inversion.** The current production model selection assumes
  citation discipline correlates with semantic correctness. A
  judge that found Qwen produces more semantically accurate prose
  while citing fewer correct chunks would force a re-evaluation
  of D015. We do not expect this finding; we record it as the
  shape of evidence that would change our mind.

- **A larger, multi-author benchmark that shifts the within-
  document gap to 2pp.** If a 150-question multi-author benchmark
  shrinks the within-document Gemma-Qwen gap from the current 5pp
  to ~2pp, the latency-vs-quality trade-off shifts substantially
  in Qwen's favour, and D015 is worth revisiting.

- **A multi-seed run that shows >3pp variance per cell.** This
  would invalidate the precise-digit reading of every result in
  Section 6 and require the report to re-state findings with
  calibrated precision. The qualitative findings would survive;
  the quantitative claims would not.

These are the four findings the project would weight heavily as
evidence against current claims. None are expected; all are
specifically falsifiable.

---

## Limitations at a Glance {#sec-limits-glance}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Retrieval ceiling** | 25% miss rate on N=70 answerable subset; caps headline at 0.598 against a 0.798 answer-stage ceiling |
| **Cross-document weakness** | All cells below 0.25 mean citation_recall on n=10 cross_document subset; not a supported primary use case |
| **Latency** | 22.9s mean answerable on production default; not suitable for interactive workflows |
| **Citation scoring** | Structural proxy only — does not measure whether prose accurately reflects cited chunks |
| **Run variance** | Single-seed runs; precise digits past first decimal should be read with caveat |
| **Benchmark coverage** | Hand-crafted by single author; failure modes not anticipated are not tested |
| **Model coverage** | Two-model survey only; D015 is within-this-survey, not a generalisation |
| **Corpus coverage** | 6 documents, 4 issuers, all English, all public; no internal documents indexed in v1 |
| **Retractions on record** | 2 — family-axis (Day 2) and within-document parity (N=40 → N=70) |
| **v2 Priority 1** | Close retrieval ceiling via HyDE or cross-encoder reranker (Q13) |
| **v2 Priority 2** | LLM-as-judge semantic correctness (Q10.3) |
| **v2 Priority 3** | Multi-seed variance estimates |
| **v2 Priorities 4–6** | Index synthetic documents · corpus + benchmark expansion · operational hardening |
| **What would change our mind** | Retrieval lift past 0.85 on excluding_retrieval_misses · Gemma/Qwen semantic inversion · benchmark shows 2pp gap · multi-seed variance >3pp |
